Strengthen local, national and regional innovation and production capacities in developing countries, particularly in low- and middle-income countries, including through financial and technical support and technology transfer on mutually agreed terms, cooperation with patent communities and other voluntary initiatives, such as Medicines Patent Pool, support and development, and use of innovation, such as Unitaid investment, to make quality health products available to developing countries at affordable prices, as well as by encouraging competition for generics in accordance with the United Nations road map
World Health Organization for Access to Medicines, Vaccines and Other Health Products, 2019-2023;

42. Make a collective effort to strengthen the capacity of developing countries to increase innovation in the local and regional manufacturing and production of medical countermeasures, including vaccines, treatments, diagnostic tools and other health products, for sustainability and to address delays in the global distribution of vaccines and medicines;

43. Encourage the fair and equitable sharing, in a timely manner, of the benefits arising from the use of pathogens, sequences or other material with pandemic potential through a multilateral system that takes into account national and international laws, regulations, obligations and frameworks, in accordance with work in other relevant areas carried out by other United Nations agencies and multilateral organizations, in particular the discussions of the intergovernmental negotiating body and the Working Group on Amendments to the International Health Regulations (2005) held in Geneva;

44. Encourage the intergovernmental negotiating body to conclude negotiations on a convention, agreement or other international instrument of the World Health Organization on prevention, preparedness and response to pandemics, giving priority to the need for equity, with a view to its adoption under article 19 or other provisions of the Constitution of the World Health Organization that the intergovernmental negotiating body may deem appropriate, within the time limits set out in paragraph 1 (5) of decision SSA2(5) of the World Health Assembly;

45. Encourage the Working Group on Amendments to the International Health Regulations (2005) to continue its work on the consideration of the proposed targeted amendments to the International Health Regulations (2005), in accordance with the deadlines set out in paragraph 2 (a) of decision 75(9) of the World Health Assembly;

46. Facilitate access to essential health services, products and vaccines, while promoting awareness of the risks of poor or falsified medical products and ensuring the quality and safety of health personnel services, products and practices, as well as protection against financial risks;

47. Support efforts to improve the physical and mental health, well-being and safety of health personnel, community health workers, front-line workers and other essential workers, in particular women, who manage heavy workloads, cope with patient influxes and work long hours during pandemics and other health emergencies, while taking into account the care and domestic work they perform without pay;

48. Support the provision of adequate remuneration, resources and training to health professionals, in particular professionals who are generally under-represented in health personnel, and ensure that they enjoy safe and decent working conditions with adequate protections, including priority and timely access to vaccines and personal protective equipment, and gender-sensitive labour policies, addressing the problems of under-remuneration and the wage gap between women and men, ensuring equal pay for work of equal value and protecting health workers, in particular women, from violence and harassment, including sexual harassment and sexual exploitation and abuse;

49. Continue to ensure the safe, expeditious and unhindered movement of humanitarian and medical personnel involved in pandemics and other health emergencies, as well as their means of transport, supplies and equipment, and to support, facilitate and authorize transportation and supply lines, so that such personnel can carry out their mission effectively and safely, which is to assist affected populations, and in this regard also reaffirm the need to take the necessary measures to respect and protect such personnel, hospitals and other medical facilities, in accordance with the provisions of international humanitarian law;

50. In accordance with international humanitarian law, to respect and protect, in situations of armed conflict, medical and humanitarian personnel whose activities are exclusively medical, their means of transport and equipment, and hospitals and other medical facilities, which are illegal to attack, and to ensure that the injured and the sick receive, as far as possible and as soon as possible, the necessary medical care and attention;

51. Commitment to strengthen the full, effective and constructive participation of women in the responsibilities and decisions of pandemics and other health emergencies at all levels, and to mainstream gender issues in all policies and programmes, including in budget response measures;

52. Ensure universal access to sexual and reproductive health care by 2030, including family planning, information and education, and the integration of reproductive health issues into national strategies and policies, as a fundamental component of universal health coverage, while reaffirming commitments to ensure universal access to sexual and reproductive health care and the enjoyment of reproductive rights by all, in accordance with the Programme of Action of the International Conference on Population and Development and the Beijing Platform for Action and the outcome documents of subsequent review conferences;

53. Accelerate action to address the global shortage of health workers, including by addressing its root causes, in accordance with the 2030 Global Strategy on Human Resources for Health, by investing in education, training, employment, retention and institutional capacity-building for the governance, leadership and planning of health personnel, and by protecting all health workers, in particular women, from all forms of violence, attacks, harassment and discriminatory practices, while noting that the safety of health workers and patient safety are interrelated;

54. Let us be guided by target 3.c of the 2030 Agenda and the World Health Organization's Global Code of Practice for the International Recruitment of Health Personnel in order to intensify cooperation in the recruitment, training, development and retention of qualified health personnel, a major component of a strong and resilient health system, in the context of prevention and preparedness strategies for health emergencies, noting with concern that the international departures of health professionals who have undergone extensive training and are highly competent are increasing, thereby weakening the health systems of their countries of origin, while taking into account the individual rights of health personnel to work in any country in accordance with applicable laws, and taking into account that nothing can be interpreted as restricting the freedom of health personnel to emigrate to countries that wish to employ them;

55. Exploit the potential of the multilateral system and engage relevant entities of the United Nations development system, within their mandates and through coordinated activities, primarily by the World Health Organization, which provides the leadership and coordination of international health action in accordance with its constitution, as well as the resident coordinator system and the revitalized United Nations country teams, acting within their mandates, and also other relevant global actors, including international financial institutions and development banks, civil society, the private sector and academia, to assist countries, in particular developing countries, in their efforts to strengthen and ensure the prevention, preparedness and response to pandemics at the national level, taking into account national context, priorities and expertise;

56. Reaffirm the commitment to provide the World Health Organization with sustainable, adequate and predictable funding to enable it to have the resources it needs to carry out its essential functions as defined in its constitution, noting the importance of transformation, improved transparency, accountability and efficiency gains in the World Health Organization, and stressing the usefulness and importance of adequate funding for the World Health Organization Emergency Contingency Reserve Fund so that it can respond promptly to health emergencies;

57. Ensure a multisectoral approach to prevention, preparedness and response to pandemics, taking into account the multifaceted causes and consequences of pandemics, including their potential impact on social protection, education, agriculture, environment, trade, travel, tourism, development and other sectors at all levels;

58. Recognize the need to strengthen, through international cooperation and global solidarity, national and regional centres of expertise, such as public health laboratories, and to assist academic and research institutions, national public health institutions, including centres for disease prevention and control or their equivalents and cross-sectoral partners, to collectively strengthen their public health capacities and competencies for a predictable and timely response, by supporting already established multinational training centres, such as the regional training centres of the World Health Organization;

59. Reaffirm the importance of national ownership and the primary role of Governments at all levels in charting their own path towards pandemic prevention, preparedness and response, taking into account national context and priorities, which is essential for reducing public health risks and vulnerabilities and for effective prevention, monitoring, early warning and response to health emergencies;

60. Strengthen legislative and regulatory frameworks, promote greater policy coherence and ensure sustainable and adequate funding to conduct and evaluate high-impact policies to protect populations from pandemics and other health emergencies, taking into account the social, economic and environmental determinants of health, working in all sectors in a government-wide and societal approach that places health at the heart of all policies and involving stakeholders in appropriate, coordinated, comprehensive and integrated action and response;

61. Take measures to integrate multisectoral emergency coordination into national health systems that are strengthened and funded in a sustainable manner, linked to multiple sectors and systems, including disaster risk management systems, and promote their implementation through well-equipped and protected health emergency personnel, supported by disaggregated data, integrated analysis, research and innovation, informed by dynamic assessments and monitoring of potential health threats, vulnerabilities and functional capacities, and supported by close links with regional and global support, coordination and collaboration structures and mechanisms at all stages of the health emergency cycle for pandemic preparedness, prevention, detection and response;

62. Emphasize the importance of Governments at all levels strengthening multisectoral, science-based and evidence-based systems, monitoring and accountability, as appropriate, in order to promote effective implementation and achieve results that support current and future actions required for pandemic prevention, preparedness and response;

63. Recognize the need to strengthen stakeholder engagement, including in the prevention, preparedness and response to pandemics, and to ensure sustainability, while including those most affected by pandemics or other health emergencies, and integrate all relevant stakeholders, local communities, civil society and academia into global health governance processes, through transparent information exchange and inclusive processes;

64. Take measures to combat and address the adverse effects of health-related misinformation, disinformation, hate speech and stigma, in particular in social networks, on physical and mental health, including to overcome reluctance to immunization in the context of pandemic prevention, preparedness and response, and to build confidence in public health systems and authorities, including by strengthening education, basic education and public health awareness, while noting that effective stakeholder engagement requires access to timely, accurate and factual information and awareness-raising, including through the use of digital health tools;

65. Prioritize the prevention, preparedness and response to pandemics in national priorities, as appropriate, on a scientific basis and with full respect for human rights and development needs, adopt a comprehensive and socially focused strategy, establish universal health coverage with primary health care at its centre, as a fundamental component of the 2030 Sustainable Development Agenda, build resilient health systems capable of maintaining and accessing essential public health functions and services, support and protect health personnel, and establish social and economic support to facilitate the widespread adoption of public health measures;

66. Further intensify the fight against non-communicable diseases as part of the prevention, preparedness and response to pandemics, bearing in mind that people living with these diseases are at higher risk of suffering from serious forms of disease caused by new pathogens with pandemic potential and of being among those most affected by pandemics, including disruptions to health services;

67. Strengthen the resilience of health systems by accelerating efforts to end global HIV/AIDS, tuberculosis and malaria epidemics, for prevention, preparedness and response to pandemics, including by building on best practices and lessons learned, improving health and laboratory information systems and strengthening procurement and supply chain management systems in the fight against the VOCID-19 pandemic, and ensuring the systematic integration of HIV/AIDS, tuberculosis and malaria responses into pandemic response measures, building on national HIV/AIDS strategic plans to guide key elements of pandemic preparedness, and emphasizing the integral role of civil society and populations in strengthening public health measures and implementing response programmes;

68. Strengthen pandemic prevention, preparedness and response, including with regard to current global epidemics, such as HIV/AIDS, tuberculosis and malaria, by sharing experiences and best practices, and improve preparedness, including by enabling the diagnosis of outbreak factors among animals with zoonotic potential, and surveillance and early warning mechanisms, in order to respond as quickly and as effectively as possible to an outbreak, recognizing the need for the "One Health" approach, which promotes cooperation among the human health, animal health and plant health sectors, as well as other relevant sectors, including through enhanced cooperation and collaboration between the World Health Organization, the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations, the World Animal Health Organization and the United Nations Environment Programme;

69. Promote international cooperation and coordination in accordance with the Joint "One Health" Action Plan 2022-2026, as appropriate, to support regional, national and community capacity-building, including through the implementation of the "One Health" approach, and to allow diagnostic tools to be used to respond fully to outbreaks of epidemics among animals, the environment and the human population, both during and between pandemics;

70. Take comprehensive measures to combat antimicrobial resistance and strengthen infection control by promoting adequate access to and responsible use of antimicrobials and preserving public health and treatment effectiveness, bearing in mind that antimicrobial resistance may be an aggravating factor in pandemics and that combating this phenomenon requires high-level collaboration between sectors and among countries worldwide, and looking forward to the high-level meeting on antimicrobial resistance to be held in 2024;

71. Improve capacities for immunization, including routine immunization, and awareness-raising, including by providing evidence-based information to build confidence, acceptance, demand and spread of proven innovations in the production of knowledge and behaviour data, collaborating with all stakeholders, generating demand and combating misinformation, and expanding immunization coverage to prevent epidemics and the spread and re-emergence of communicable diseases, including vaccine-preventable and already eradicated diseases and diseases currently being eradicated;

72. Take measures to enhance the resilience of national health systems to prevent, prepare for and respond to pandemics and other health emergencies, including by integrating disaster risk management into primary, secondary and tertiary health care, especially at the local level, strengthening the capacity of health workers to understand disaster risks and implementing disaster risk reduction approaches in the health sector, promoting and improving disaster medicine training capacities, and encouraging community-based health associations and raising awareness of disaster risk reduction strategies associated with health programmes, in collaboration with other sectors and in the context of the implementation of the International Health Regulations (2005) issued by the World Health Organization, while taking into account the Bangkok Principles for the Implementation of the Health Components of the Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction (2015-2030);

73. Emphasize that financing for health requires global solidarity and collective effort, and commit to intensify international cooperation to help increase the capacity of developing countries, in particular African countries and small island developing States, including by strengthening official development assistance, financial and technical support and support for research, development and innovation programmes;

74. Ensure that domestic public expenditure on health is sufficient, where appropriate, to expand the pooling of health resources, maximize efficiency and ensure equitable distribution of health expenditures, in order to provide timely, essential, economic and quality health services, improve service coverage, reduce poverty due to health expenditures and ensure protection from related financial risks, bearing in mind that private investment may have a role to play, as appropriate;

75. Strengthen international cooperation and coordination and funding and investment commitments in support of efforts to build, strengthen and maintain pandemic prevention, preparedness and response capacities, including research and development capacities in developing countries, including through the strengthening of official development assistance and adequate relief funding for future interventions, as well as other innovative forms of financing, bearing in mind that everyone should be able to benefit from quality health care without facing financial difficulties;

76. Take advantage of existing financing tools, including multilateral development banks, to mobilize additional needs-oriented, reliable, flexible, equitable, predictable and sustainable funding for pandemic prevention, preparedness and response activities, as well as quick-release relief funds to finance responses to international public health emergencies and to strengthen funding mechanisms for the global health sector and other relevant sectors, as well as their mandated responsibilities and responsibilities;

text_fr: Dans le prolongement de la présente déclaration politique, nous :
text_en: As a follow-up to this political declaration, we will:

items_fr: 77. Prions le Secrétaire général, agissant en consultation avec l'Organisation mondiale de la Santé et les autres entités compétentes, de présenter à la soixante-dixneuvième session de l'Assemblée générale, un rapport assorti de recommandations
items_en: 77. Request the Secretary-General, in consultation with the World Health Organization and other relevant entities, to submit to the seventy-ninth session of the General Assembly a report with recommendations

text_fr: aux  fins  de  l'application  de  la  présente  déclaration  en  vue  du  renforcement  de  la prévention, de la préparation et de la riposte face aux pandémies, lequel servira de base à une réunion de haut niveau devant se tenir en 2026 ;
text_en: for the purpose of implementing this Declaration with a view to strengthening pandemic prevention, preparedness and response, which will serve as the basis for a high-level meeting to be held in 2026;

items_fr: 78. Décidons de convoquer une réunion de haut niveau sur la prévention, la préparation et la riposte face aux pandémies en 2026 à New York, en vue de procéder à un examen d'ensemble de l'application de la présente déclaration, réunion dont la portée et les modalités seront arrêtées au plus tard à la soixante-dix-neuvième session de l'Assemblée générale, compte tenu des résultats des autres initiatives  connexes mises  en  œuvre  pour  renforcer  la  prévention,  la  préparation  et  la  riposte  face  aux pandémies, et en pleine coordination avec ces initiatives.
items_en: 78. Decide to convene a high-level meeting on pandemic prevention, preparedness and response in 2026 in New York, with a view to conducting a comprehensive review of the implementation of the present declaration, the scope and modalities of which will be decided no later than the seventy-ninth session of the General Assembly, taking into account the results of other related initiatives to strengthen pandemic prevention, preparedness and response, and in full coordination with those initiatives.
